record_identity.title: Adversarial refusal-trigger poisoning of defensive artefact analysis

summary: A security-integrity failure in which an attacker embeds restricted, prohibited, alarming, or policy-sensitive material within malware, spyware, source code, documents, logs, network artefacts, or other hostile evidence so that an AI-assisted defensive system misclassifies the artefact's contents as a request to generate, facilitate, optimise, or operationalise prohibited material. The resulting safety refusal, analysis termination, model substitution, capability degradation, or workflow interruption creates an evasion pathway through which safety controls can be weaponised against defensive inspection.
why_it_matters_to_CAM: CAM requires restricted-domain classification to consider interaction context, operational applicability, optimisation potential, trajectory, and harm profile rather than isolated words or subject categories. Defensive, forensic, auditing, moderation, and incident-response tasks may necessarily contain hostile or prohibited material because that material is the object of inspection. Where a system inherits embedded artefact content as analyst intent and refuses the surrounding defensive task, it collapses the distinction between evidence and instruction, allows adversaries to manipulate the safety boundary, and converts a protective control into part of the attack surface.

source_records.source_title: X post reporting nuclear- and biological-weapons text embedded in spyware to trigger LLM safety refusals
source_records.deployment_context: Public report concerning adversarial spyware or malware evasion of AI-assisted defensive analysis.
source_records.source_context: The post reports that malware developers inserted nuclear- and biological-weapons text into spyware with the objective of triggering LLM safety refusals so that an AI security scanner would not analyse the artefact. The originating technical report, named malware sample, vendor, scanner, and independent forensic evidence were not recovered during the present research pass.
source_records.relevance_note: Primary observation prompting the record. The specific spyware implementation remains provisional pending recovery of the underlying technical source.

source_records.source_title: Defensive Refusal Bias: How Safety Alignment Fails Cyber Defenders
source_records.author_or_publisher: David Campbell, Neil Kale, Udari Madhushani Sehwag, Bert Herring, Nick Price, Dan Borges, Alex Levinson, and Christina Q Knight
source_records.deployment_context: Evaluation of legitimate defensive cybersecurity tasks derived from National Collegiate Cyber Defense Competition examples.
source_records.source_context: The study evaluated 2,390 defensive cybersecurity examples and found that security-sensitive language increased refusal rates by 2.72 times compared with semantically equivalent neutral requests. Malware-analysis tasks recorded a refusal rate of 34.3 percent, and explicit declarations of authorisation did not reliably prevent refusal.
source_records.relevance_note: Provides direct empirical support for safety-mediated denial of legitimate defensive cybersecurity assistance and demonstrates that sensitive vocabulary can override task legitimacy.

source_records.source_title: Prompt Injection Evaluations: Refusal Boundary Instability and Artifact-Dependent Compliance in GPT-4-Series Models
source_records.deployment_context: Evaluation of refusal stability across 3,274 structured prompt and artefact perturbation runs.
source_records.source_context: The study found that refusal behaviour was probabilistic, unstable, and dependent on artefact type. Textual artefacts such as ransomware notes exhibited materially different refusal-boundary behaviour from executable malware artefacts, showing that the representation and contents of an inspected artefact can alter the model's safety response.
source_records.relevance_note: Supports the claim that artefact contents and artefact type can alter safety-boundary behaviour independently of the surrounding analyst's legitimate defensive objective.

source_records.source_title: The Promptware Kill Chain: How Prompt Injections Gradually Evolved Into a Multi-Step Malware
source_records.author_or_publisher: Ben Nassi, Bruce Schneier, and Oleg Brodt
source_records.deployment_context: Threat modelling of multi-stage adversarial campaigns targeting LLM-integrated systems.
source_records.source_context: The paper describes prompt injection as part of a broader promptware attack class capable of initial access, privilege escalation, persistence, lateral movement, and actions on objective. It supports treating adversarial manipulation of LLM context as a structured security threat rather than a simple content-policy anomaly.
source_records.relevance_note: Supports classification of attacker-controlled model manipulation as a security-integrity and adversarial-system threat.

system_context.system_type: LLM-assisted malware analysis, threat detection, security scanning, forensic analysis, moderation, or incident-response system
system_context.specific_model_or_runtime: Unspecified large language model integrated into an AI-assisted security scanner or defensive analysis workflow
system_context.interface_surface: malware scanner, file-analysis pipeline, source-code inspection system, forensic analysis interface, security operations workflow, agentic defensive system, or artefact-ingestion pipeline
system_context.interaction_mode: analysis of hostile files, malware, spyware, source code, documents, logs, network artefacts, indicators of compromise, or other untrusted evidence
system_context.deployment_context: Cybersecurity, threat intelligence, malware analysis, incident response, digital forensics, vulnerability research, platform moderation, and autonomous or semi-autonomous defensive operations.
system_context.user_role: security analyst, malware researcher, incident responder, forensic examiner, threat-intelligence researcher, platform-integrity investigator, developer, auditor, or autonomous defensive agent
system_context.affected_population: Security practitioners, researchers, organisations, infrastructure operators, platform-integrity teams, and users who depend on AI-assisted systems to inspect hostile or untrusted artefacts

jurisdictional_context.sector: cybersecurity / threat intelligence / digital forensics / AI-assisted security infrastructure
jurisdictional_context.jurisdictional_assessment: This record does not assert a final legal breach or attribute the reported spyware conduct to a named developer, vendor, state, or operator. It documents a security and governance failure pattern in which attackers may exploit LLM safety controls to disrupt or evade legitimate defensive analysis.

failure_mode_definition: A failure mode in which hostile or untrusted artefact content activates an AI system's safety, refusal, policy, or restricted-domain controls in a manner that suppresses the surrounding defensive task. The system fails to preserve the distinction between content presented as an object of inspection and content requested for generation, optimisation, facilitation, execution, or operational use. An attacker can therefore insert refusal-triggering language, restricted-domain material, policy-sensitive terminology, apparent instructions, or other classifier-targeting content into malware, spyware, files, documents, repositories, logs, communications, or network artefacts to induce refusal, truncation, model substitution, capability degradation, or termination of defensive analysis.
failure_threshold: The threshold is met where hostile or untrusted content contained inside an artefact materially changes an AI system's willingness or ability to inspect, classify, explain, summarise, extract indicators from, support containment of, or otherwise defensively analyse that artefact, despite the surrounding task being forensic, defensive, auditing, moderation, safety, integrity, or incident-response oriented. The failure does not require the embedded material to function as a conventional imperative instruction. It is sufficient that the content predictably activates refusal, policy restriction, model downgrade, analysis termination, or another safety-mediated denial of defensive capability.

failure_classification.likelihood: plausible and supported by documented defensive-refusal and artefact-dependent safety-boundary behaviour; specific reported exploitation remains provisional
failure_classification.failure_scope: cross-system / adversarial / potentially systemic across LLM-assisted security and analysis tools
failure_classification.recurrence_pattern: The specific spyware report is currently a single public observation, while the enabling conditions are supported by broader empirical evidence of defensive refusal bias, artefact-dependent refusal boundaries, and adversarial manipulation of LLM context.
failure_classification.taxonomy_reference: CAM-EQ2026-OPERATIONS-003-SUP-01 Appendix B — FF.SECURITY_INTEGRITY; incident-specific classification remains FCS.PROVISIONAL pending recovery of the originating technical report
failure_classification.persistence: potentially persistent wherever LLM-assisted defensive systems ingest attacker-controlled content without preserving an enforceable analytical separation between artefact data and governing instructions
failure_classification.reproducibility: The precise reported spyware sample has not been recovered; the underlying enabling behaviour is experimentally reproducible through security-sensitive defensive prompts, artefact-dependent refusal tests, and hostile content inserted into LLM context.
failure_classification.visibility: public social-platform report, academic cybersecurity evaluations, user-facing refusals, model substitutions, truncated analysis, scanner blind spots, and failed defensive workflows

triage.mitigation_status: unresolved — existing prompt-injection, restricted-domain, and defensive-analysis controls provide partial coverage, but an explicit analytical-artefact separation rule and adversarial refusal-trigger handling requirement have not yet been confirmed across CAM.
triage.recommended_next_step: Continue source recovery for the originating spyware or malware report; preserve the X post and screenshot as the initiating observation; evaluate CAM for an explicit Analytical Artefact and Embedded-Content Separation rule; and test representative LLM-assisted security systems using benign defensive tasks containing embedded restricted-domain and refusal-triggering material.

distinguishing_observations.observation: The reported incident and the general failure class have different evidence states.
distinguishing_observations.detail: The X post provides a direct public report of a specific spyware technique, but the underlying malware sample, scanner, model, vendor, technical report, and independent forensic verification remain unrecovered. The broader failure class is nevertheless supported by published research on defensive refusal bias, artefact-dependent refusal behaviour, and promptware.

distinguishing_observations.observation: The attack is adjacent to but not identical to conventional indirect prompt injection.
distinguishing_observations.detail: The attacker may insert an instruction intended to manipulate the model, but the attack may also work through non-imperative policy-sensitive content whose mere presence activates a refusal or restricted-domain classifier. The record therefore covers instruction poisoning, classifier poisoning, policy-trigger poisoning, and refusal-trigger poisoning.

distinguishing_observations.observation: The presence of prohibited material may be necessary evidence.
distinguishing_observations.detail: Malware, extremist material, abuse reports, exploit code, ransomware notes, biological-threat terminology, and other dangerous content may need to be inspected precisely because they are hostile. Their presence inside an artefact does not establish that the analyst requested or endorsed them.

distinguishing_observations.observation: The failure does not require unrestricted model compliance.
distinguishing_observations.detail: A model may still withhold transformations that would improve malware, optimise a weapon, facilitate exploitation, enable evasion, or otherwise create material harmful capability uplift while continuing to classify, explain, extract indicators, support containment, and provide defensive remediation.

distinguishing_observations.observation: Safety controls become part of the attack surface when attackers can invoke them.
distinguishing_observations.detail: A protective boundary ceases to function solely as a safeguard when hostile actors can deliberately trigger it to blind scanners, interrupt analysis, remove defensive capabilities, or force model substitution.

user_impact: Security analysts may be prevented from inspecting malware or spyware because the artefact contains terms or passages associated with prohibited domains.
Autonomous defensive agents may fail silently because they cannot rephrase, appeal, or independently distinguish an attacker-induced refusal from a genuine policy boundary.
Malware developers may gain an evasion pathway requiring little or no alteration to the malicious code's functional behaviour.
Incident-response and forensic workflows may lose critical indicators, behavioural explanations, extraction results, or containment guidance.
Organisations may overestimate the coverage of AI-assisted security scanners whose safety layer can be manipulated by attacker-controlled content.
Critical infrastructure, public institutions, journalists, civil-society researchers, and high-risk targets may experience delayed detection or impaired investigation of sophisticated spyware.
Users may be unable to determine whether an analysis failed because the artefact was benign, encrypted, unsupported, policy-restricted, adversarially poisoned, or outside the model's capability.

existing_cam_coverage.instrument: CAM-BS2025-AEON-006-SCH-07 — Annex E: Restricted Domain Engagement & Verification
existing_cam_coverage.coverage_type: restricted-domain context, trajectory, operational applicability, optimisation, and harm-profile classification
existing_cam_coverage.relevance: Requires restricted-domain engagement to be graduated rather than binary, prohibits keyword-only classification, protects legitimate safety and integrity inquiry, and requires calibrated balancing between non-proliferation and legitimate investigation.
existing_cam_coverage.gap_remaining: Does not yet appear to state with sufficient specificity that prohibited or restricted content embedded within an artefact must remain classified as an analytical object and must not be inherited as the intent of the analyst or surrounding defensive task.

existing_cam_coverage.instrument: CAM-BS2025-AEON-006-SCH-06 — Annex E: Refusal & Boundary Expression Schedule
existing_cam_coverage.coverage_type: graduated refusal, conditional access, constraint disclosure, and preservation of safe continuation paths
existing_cam_coverage.relevance: Supports constrained analysis rather than complete termination and requires limitations to be expressed without treating the legitimate analyst as a wrongdoer.
existing_cam_coverage.gap_remaining: Refusal-expression controls operate after engagement posture has been resolved and do not independently prevent hostile artefact contents from corrupting the prior classification decision.

existing_cam_coverage.instrument: CAM-EQ2026-SECURITY-001-PLATINUM — Security, Integrity & Adversarial Resilience Charter
existing_cam_coverage.coverage_type: adversarial resilience, integrity state, trust gradient, and security-boundary protection
existing_cam_coverage.relevance: Provides the primary security basis for treating attacker-controlled artefact content as an integrity threat rather than as trusted governing context.
existing_cam_coverage.gap_remaining: A direct cross-reference to refusal-trigger poisoning and analytical-object separation may be required.

existing_cam_coverage.coverage_type: boundary failure, transformation risk, diffusion risk, and adversarial content handling
existing_cam_coverage.relevance: Supports separation between hostile input, system interpretation, transformation, and downstream action.
existing_cam_coverage.gap_remaining: Existing boundary controls should be checked for explicit treatment of hostile content intended to trigger safety withdrawal rather than unsafe model compliance.

existing_cam_coverage.instrument: CAM-EQ2026-OPERATIONS-003-SUP-01 — Appendix B: Runtime & Governance Failure Taxonomy
existing_cam_coverage.relevance: Routes the failure primarily through FF.SECURITY_INTEGRITY with related FF.CLASSIFICATION, FF.EXECUTION, FF.GOVERNANCE, FF.INFRASTRUCTURE_CONTINUITY, and FF.UX_REPRESENTATION effects.
existing_cam_coverage.gap_remaining: The taxonomy can classify the failure but does not itself establish the required runtime separation between hostile artefact content and operator intent.

governance_gap: CAM substantially covers contextual restricted-domain classification, adversarial integrity, graduated refusal, defensive continuity, and failure routing. The remaining gap is an explicit rule requiring systems to distinguish hostile content presented as an object of inspection from content requested for generation or operational use. Without this separation, attacker-controlled artefacts may inherit authority over the model's safety posture and terminate the very analysis intended to detect them.
repair_hypothesis: Introduce an Analytical Artefact and Embedded-Content Separation rule requiring AI systems to preserve the surrounding defensive objective when ingesting hostile evidence. Restricted or prohibited artefact contents should be treated as untrusted data and potential adversarial indicators, not as analyst intent or governing instructions. The system should continue classification, indicator extraction, behavioural analysis, containment support, and defensive remediation while withholding only transformations that would materially increase harmful operational capability.

recommended_repair_path.repair_action: Separate artefact content from operator intent.
recommended_repair_path.expected_control: Restricted or prohibited material found inside an artefact must remain attributed to the artefact unless independent interaction evidence supports attribution to the analyst.
recommended_repair_path.implementation_note: Do not infer harmful analyst intent from malware strings, source-code comments, documents, embedded prompts, logs, extracted text, or other hostile evidence.

recommended_repair_path.repair_action: Treat refusal-triggering content as an adversarial signal.
recommended_repair_path.expected_control: Content apparently designed to activate safety refusal, policy restriction, model substitution, or analysis termination should increase security scrutiny rather than terminate inspection.
recommended_repair_path.implementation_note: Record suspected classifier manipulation, prompt injection, policy-trigger poisoning, or refusal-trigger poisoning as an indicator of compromise or evasion.

recommended_repair_path.expected_control: Continue safe classification, behavioural explanation, indicator extraction, containment guidance, and remediation support.
recommended_repair_path.implementation_note: Withhold only outputs that materially optimise malware, facilitate exploitation, increase evasion capability, operationalise prohibited systems, or otherwise create harmful capability uplift.

recommended_repair_path.repair_action: Apply least privilege and execution separation.
recommended_repair_path.expected_control: The analysis model should not receive unnecessary execution authority, secrets, credentials, or direct control of downstream systems.
recommended_repair_path.implementation_note: Contain residual model confusion so that hostile artefact content cannot produce unsafe actions even where analytical classification fails.

recommended_repair_path.expected_control: Where analysis is truncated, rerouted, or degraded, expose the controlling condition and preserve an escalation or human-review pathway.
recommended_repair_path.implementation_note: Do not silently report an artefact as clean, unsupported, or unanalysable when safety controls caused the failure.

recommended_repair_path.expected_control: Evaluate whether embedded high-risk terminology, prohibited-domain text, false instructions, policy quotations, and classifier-targeting strings can blind or degrade analysis.
recommended_repair_path.implementation_note: Testing should cover text, source code, binaries, extracted strings, images, documents, archives, logs, repositories, memory, retrieval systems, and multimodal inputs.

affected_instruments: CAM-BS2025-AEON-006-SCH-07 — Annex E: Restricted Domain Engagement & Verification, §§3, 4, 4.1, 6.2, 6.6, 8, 9.1 and 9.2
CAM-BS2025-AEON-006-SCH-06 — Annex E: Refusal & Boundary Expression Schedule, §§6.1–6.2
CAM-EQ2026-SECURITY-001-PLATINUM — Security, Integrity & Adversarial Resilience Charter
CAM-EQ2026-OPERATIONS-003-SUP-01 — Appendix B: Runtime & Governance Failure Taxonomy, FF and FCS
affected_annexes: Annex E — Ethical Legitimacy & Civilisational Floor
cam_internal.governance_layer: adversarial artefact handling, restricted-domain classification, defensive-analysis continuity, and security-boundary integrity
cam_internal.routing_note: Primary routing is FF.SECURITY_INTEGRITY, with related FF.CLASSIFICATION, FF.EXECUTION, FF.GOVERNANCE, FF.INFRASTRUCTURE_CONTINUITY, and FF.UX_REPRESENTATION effects. The initiating spyware report remains FCS.PROVISIONAL pending recovery of its originating technical evidence. The general failure type is corroborated by published research on defensive refusal bias, artefact-dependent refusal boundaries, and promptware.
cam_internal.cam_failure_type: adversarial refusal-trigger poisoning of defensive artefact analysis
cam_internal.cam_compliance_status: non-compliant where hostile or untrusted evidence is treated as though its embedded restricted content were requested, endorsed, or operationally intended by the analyst, causing refusal, model substitution, capability degradation, or termination of legitimate defensive analysis
cam_internal.cam_internal_failure_statement: Under CAM, restricted-domain classification must resolve the surrounding task, interaction trajectory, operational applicability, optimisation potential, and harm profile. A system that inherits hostile artefact content as analyst intent and terminates defensive inspection allows safety controls to become an attacker-controlled evasion surface.
cam_internal.cam_expected_control: Preserve a strict analytical distinction between artefact content and operator intent; treat embedded model-directed, policy-sensitive, or refusal-triggering material as untrusted evidence; maintain constrained defensive analysis; withhold only transformations that create material harmful capability uplift; and surface attempted safety-trigger exploitation as an adversarial indicator.
cam_internal.cam_observed_failure: A public report states that spyware developers inserted nuclear- and biological-weapons text into malicious artefacts so an LLM-based security scanner would refuse analysis. The specific case remains provisional because the originating technical report and malware evidence have not yet been recovered. The underlying failure pathway is supported by external research showing defensive refusal bias, artefact-dependent safety boundaries, and adversarial manipulation of LLM context.
cam_internal.recommended_cam_action: Add an Analytical Artefact and Embedded-Content Separation rule to CAM-BS2025-AEON-006-SCH-07 and cross-reference it from the relevant SECURITY boundary and adversarial-input provisions. The rule should expressly cover conventional prompt injection, policy-trigger poisoning, refusal-trigger poisoning, classifier manipulation, and other hostile content intended to suppress defensive analysis.

repair_status.monitoring_status: active triage / explicit corpus gap remains
repair_status.verification_note: Source-authority separation and graduated restricted-domain doctrine permit hostile content to be treated as evidence rather than instruction and preserve defensive contextual analysis. The corpus still lacks an explicit Analytical Artefact and Embedded-Content Separation rule covering refusal-trigger and policy-trigger poisoning.
remaining_gaps: No explicit Analytical Artefact and Embedded-Content Separation primitive is defined.
No explicit adversarial refusal-trigger or policy-trigger poisoning handling rule is defined.
The originating spyware sample and technical report remain unrecovered.

ecosystem_status.basis: The specific spyware report is currently a single public observation, while the enabling conditions are supported by broader empirical evidence of defensive refusal bias, artefact-dependent refusal boundaries, and adversarial manipulation of LLM context.

corpus_coverage.coverage_summary: Source-authority separation and graduated restricted-domain doctrine permit hostile content to be treated as evidence rather than instruction and preserve defensive contextual analysis. The corpus still lacks an explicit Analytical Artefact and Embedded-Content Separation rule covering refusal-trigger and policy-trigger poisoning.
remaining_gaps: No explicit Analytical Artefact and Embedded-Content Separation primitive is defined.
No explicit adversarial refusal-trigger or policy-trigger poisoning handling rule is defined.
The originating spyware sample and technical report remain unrecovered.
